root_text.an5.200:1.3: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno kāmaṁ manasikaroto kāmesu cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati na vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:1.4: Nekkhammaṁ kho panassa manasikaroto nekkhamme cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:1.5: Tassa taṁ cittaṁ sugataṁ subhāvitaṁ suvuṭṭhitaṁ suvimuttaṁ suvisaṁyuttaṁ kāmehi;
root_text.an5.200:1.6: ye ca kāmapaccayā uppajjanti āsavā vighātapariḷāhā, mutto so tehi, na so taṁ vedanaṁ vediyati.
root_text.an5.200:2.1: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno byāpādaṁ manasikaroto byāpāde cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati na vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:2.2: Abyāpādaṁ kho panassa manasikaroto abyāpāde cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:2.3: Tassa taṁ cittaṁ sugataṁ subhāvitaṁ suvuṭṭhitaṁ suvimuttaṁ suvisaṁyuttaṁ byāpādena;
root_text.an5.200:2.4: ye ca byāpādapaccayā uppajjanti āsavā vighātapariḷāhā, mutto so tehi, na so taṁ vedanaṁ vediyati.
root_text.an5.200:3.1: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno vihesaṁ manasikaroto vihesāya cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati na vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:3.2: Avihesaṁ kho panassa manasikaroto avihesāya cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:3.3: Tassa taṁ cittaṁ sugataṁ subhāvitaṁ suvuṭṭhitaṁ suvimuttaṁ suvisaṁyuttaṁ vihesāya;
root_text.an5.200:3.4: ye ca vihesāpaccayā uppajjanti āsavā vighātapariḷāhā, mutto so tehi, na so taṁ vedanaṁ vediyati.
root_text.an5.200:4.1: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno rūpaṁ manasikaroto rūpe cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati na vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:4.2: Arūpaṁ kho panassa manasikaroto arūpe cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:4.3: Tassa taṁ cittaṁ sugataṁ subhāvitaṁ suvuṭṭhitaṁ suvimuttaṁ suvisaṁyuttaṁ rūpehi;
root_text.an5.200:4.4: ye ca rūpapaccayā uppajjanti āsavā vighātapariḷāhā, mutto so tehi, na so taṁ vedanaṁ vediyati.
root_text.an5.200:5.1: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno sakkāyaṁ manasikaroto sakkāye cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati na vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:5.2: Sakkāyanirodhaṁ kho panassa manasikaroto sakkāyanirodhe cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati vimuccati.
root_text.an5.200:5.3: Tassa taṁ cittaṁ sugataṁ subhāvitaṁ suvuṭṭhitaṁ suvimuttaṁ suvisaṁyuttaṁ sakkāyena;
root_text.an5.200:5.4: ye ca sakkāyapaccayā uppajjanti āsavā vighātapariḷāhā, mutto so tehi, na so taṁ vedanaṁ vediyati.
root_text.an5.200:6.1: Tassa kāmanandīpi nānuseti, byāpādanandīpi nānuseti, vihesānandīpi nānuseti, rūpanandīpi nānuseti, sakkāyanandīpi nānuseti so kāmanandiyāpi ananusayā, byāpādanandiyāpi ananusayā, vihesānandiyāpi ananusayā, rūpanandiyāpi ananusayā, sakkāyanandiyāpi ananusayā.
root_text.an5.200:6.2: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu niranusayo, acchecchi taṇhaṁ, vivattayi saṁyojanaṁ, sammā mānābhisamayā antamakāsi dukkhassa.
root_text.an5.200:6.3: Imā kho, bhikkhave, pañca nissāraṇīyā dhātuyo”ti.

translation_text.an5.200:1.1: “Mendicants, there are these five elements of escape.
translation_text.an5.200:1.3: Take a case where a mendicant focuses on sensual pleasures, but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided.
translation_text.an5.200:1.4: But when they focus on renunciation, on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided.
translation_text.an5.200:1.5: Their mind is in a good state, well developed, well risen, well freed, and well detached from sensual pleasures.
translation_text.an5.200:1.6: They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of sensual pleasures, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling.
translation_text.an5.200:1.7: This is how the escape from sensual pleasures is explained.
translation_text.an5.200:2.1: Take another case where a mendicant focuses on ill will, but their mind does not leap forth …
translation_text.an5.200:2.2: But when they focus on good will, their mind leaps forth …
translation_text.an5.200:2.3: Their mind is in a good state … well detached from ill will.
translation_text.an5.200:2.4: They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of ill will, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling.
translation_text.an5.200:2.5: This is how the escape from ill will is explained.
translation_text.an5.200:3.1: Take another case where a mendicant focuses on harming, but their mind does not leap forth …
translation_text.an5.200:3.2: But when they focus on compassion, their mind leaps forth …
translation_text.an5.200:3.3: Their mind is in a good state … well detached from harming.
translation_text.an5.200:3.4: They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of harming, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling.
translation_text.an5.200:3.5: This is how the escape from harming is explained.
translation_text.an5.200:4.1: Take another case where a mendicant focuses on form, but their mind does not leap forth …
translation_text.an5.200:4.2: But when they focus on the formless, their mind leaps forth …
translation_text.an5.200:4.3: Their mind is in a good state … well detached from forms.
translation_text.an5.200:4.4: They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of form, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling.
translation_text.an5.200:4.5: This is how the escape from forms is explained.
translation_text.an5.200:5.1: Take a case where a mendicant focuses on substantial reality, but their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided.
translation_text.an5.200:5.2: But when they focus on the cessation of substantial reality, their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided.
translation_text.an5.200:5.3: Their mind is in a good state, well developed, well risen, well freed, and well detached from substantial reality.
translation_text.an5.200:5.4: They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of substantial reality, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling.
translation_text.an5.200:5.5: This is how the escape from substantial reality is explained.
translation_text.an5.200:6.1: Delight in sensual pleasures, ill will, harming, form, and substantial reality don’t linger within them.
translation_text.an5.200:6.2: That’s why they’re called a mendicant who is without underlying tendencies, who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering.
translation_text.an5.200:6.3: These are the five elements of escape.”

variant_text.an5.200:6.2: acchecchi → acchejji (sya-all, km, mr) | vivattayi → vāvattayi (bj); vivaṭṭayi (sya-all, pts1ed)
